metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Pre-calculus 11 is the primary academic mathematics course for students planning to study mathematics, sciences, or engineering at the post-secondary level. Students extend algebraic skills to rational exponents, radicals, polynomial factoring, rational expressions, quadratic functions, inequalities, and trigonometry including non-right triangle applications. This course prepares students for Pre-calculus 12 and, ultimately, Calculus.
metadata.general_description: A rigorous senior mathematics course covering real numbers, rational exponents, radical operations, polynomial and rational expressions, quadratic and linear-quadratic systems, inequalities, and non-right triangle trigonometry — preparing students for Pre-calculus 12 and post-secondary STEM pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Algebra allows us to generalize relationships through abstract thinking.
big_ideas.elaboration: Algebraic reasoning allows us to describe and analyze mathematical relationships generally — across domains including real numbers, rational expressions, and quadratic functions.

big_ideas.statement: The meanings of, and connections between, operations extend to powers, radicals, and polynomials.
big_ideas.elaboration: The operations of arithmetic extend naturally to radicals and rational exponents — maintaining the same structural properties and enabling new algebraic capabilities essential for calculus.

big_ideas.statement: Quadratic relationships are prevalent in the world around us.
big_ideas.elaboration: Quadratic functions model a wide range of real phenomena — from projectile motion and engineering design to profit maximization — making them one of the most practically important function families.

big_ideas.statement: Trigonometry involves using proportional reasoning to solve indirect measurement problems.
big_ideas.elaboration: Extending trigonometry beyond right triangles — using the sine and cosine laws — allows indirect measurement of any triangle, with applications in navigation, surveying, and engineering.

competencies.raw: Develop thinking strategies to solve puzzles and play games; explore, analyze, and apply mathematical ideas using reason, technology, and other tools; estimate reasonably and demonstrate fluent, flexible, and strategic thinking about number; model with mathematics in situational contexts; think creatively and with curiosity and wonder when exploring problems.
i_can_statements: I can develop algebraic thinking strategies to solve complex polynomial, radical, and trigonometric problems.
I can use graphing technology to explore quadratic functions and trigonometric relationships.
I can estimate solutions to quadratic and radical equations and judge their reasonableness.
I can model real-world situations using quadratic functions and trigonometry.

competencies.raw: Develop, demonstrate, and apply conceptual understanding through play, story, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore and illustrate mathematical concepts and relationships; apply flexible and strategic approaches to solve problems; solve with persistence and positive disposition; engage in problem-solving connected with place, story, cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can develop and demonstrate understanding of quadratic functions, radicals, and trigonometry through inquiry.
I can visualize parabolas, the unit circle, and radical functions to build conceptual understanding.
I can apply multiple strategies — factoring, the quadratic formula, completing the square — to solve quadratic equations.

competencies.raw: Explain and justify mathematical ideas and decisions in many ways; represent mathematical ideas in concrete, pictorial, and symbolic forms; use mathematical vocabulary and language; take risks when offering ideas in classroom discourse.
i_can_statements: I can explain and justify algebraic transformations, factoring steps, and solution processes.
I can represent functions in multiple forms: graph, table, equation, and verbal description.
I can use precise algebraic vocabulary including domain, range, vertex, axis of symmetry, and discriminant.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect mathematical concepts with each other, with other areas, and with personal interests; use mistakes as opportunities to advance learning; incorporate First Peoples worldviews, perspectives, knowledge, and practices.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my algebraic thinking and identify and correct my misconceptions.
I can connect quadratic functions to physics (projectile motion) and engineering applications.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical knowledge and perspectives into my thinking.

areas.name: Real Number System and Rational Exponents
areas.raw: Real number system; powers with rational exponents; radical operations and equations.
topics: Classification of real numbers: natural, whole, integer, rational, irrational
Rational exponents: converting between radical and exponent form (x^(m/n) = (ⁿ√x)^m)
Exponent laws applied to rational exponents
Simplifying radical expressions: like radicals, multiplication and division of radicals
Operations with radicals: adding, subtracting, multiplying, dividing, and rationalizing denominators
Solving radical equations algebraically and graphically; identifying extraneous roots
Domain restrictions for radical functions

areas.name: Polynomial and Rational Expressions
areas.raw: Polynomial factoring; rational expressions and equations.
topics: Factoring strategies: GCF, trinomials (ax²+bx+c), difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes
Rational expressions: simplifying, identifying non-permissible values
Operations on rational expressions: multiplication, division, addition, subtraction
Solving rational equations: finding extraneous roots
Applications: rate and work problems using rational equations

areas.name: Quadratic Functions and Equations
areas.raw: Quadratic functions and equations; linear and quadratic inequalities.
topics: Quadratic functions: standard form y = ax²+bx+c, vertex form y = a(x-h)²+k, factored form
Completing the square: converting to vertex form
Graph characteristics: vertex, axis of symmetry, direction of opening, max/min, domain, range
Solving quadratic equations: factoring, completing the square, quadratic formula
Discriminant: determining number and nature of roots (b²-4ac)
Contextual quadratic applications: projectile motion, area, profit maximization
Linear and quadratic inequalities: solving and graphing solution sets on number lines

areas.name: Trigonometry
areas.raw: Non-right triangle trigonometry; standard position angles and unit circle.
topics: Angles in standard position: initial arm, terminal arm, reference angle, coterminal angles
Special angles: 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° — exact values from unit circle
Unit circle: coordinates of points on the unit circle
Sine and cosine laws: derivation and application to non-right triangles
Ambiguous case of the sine law (SSA)
Applications: navigation, surveying, and indirect measurement problems

areas.name: Financial Literacy
areas.raw: Financial literacy: compound interest, investments and loans with regular payments, buy/lease.
topics: Compound interest: A = P(1+r/n)^(nt) — calculating future value and present value
Regular payment plans: savings plans and loan amortization (using technology)
Comparing investment and loan options
Buy vs. lease analysis for vehicles and equipment
